summary: Jeff Atwood shares lessons learned from building Stack Overflow with a distributed team, arguing that remote development can work but requires specific conditions and infrastructure. He discovered that coding alone was untenable and that having a coding buddy was essential for motivation and direction. The post outlines prerequisites for remote work: team members who genuinely love code, proven track records of excellence, and strong autonomous leadership with clear vision. Atwood then presents advice from Miguel de Icaza on scaling remote teams, emphasizing real-time chat, persistent mailing lists, and regular voice communication. He advocates for lightweight process including weekly status reports and meeting minutes. Despite the challenges, Atwood concludes that remote development represents the future of work and is worth the effort to figure out.
key_points: Working remotely alone as a programmer leads to paralysis and lack of motivation; the minimum viable team size is two
Remote work only succeeds with proven, passionate programmers who have track records of excellence - not newbies or those 'phoning it in'
Three essential tools for scaling remote teams: real-time chat, persistent mailing lists, and voice/video communication
Lightweight process matters: weekly status reports covering what was done, what's planned, and blockers, plus meeting minutes distributed to the team
Remote development requires more discipline than co-located work but represents the future of intellectual work
money_quotes: As programmers go, I'm fairly social. Which still means I'm a borderline sociopath by normal standards.
Don't even think about working remotely with anyone who doesn't freakin' bleed ones and zeros, and has a proven track record of getting things done.
Newbie programmers, or competent programmers who are phoning it in, are absolutely not going to have the moxie necessary to get things done remotely.
I know, I know, the whole reason we got into this programming thing was to avoid talking to other people, but bear with me here.
Noisy, argumentative, or useless things posted to the mailing list should be punishable by death. Or noogies.
Remote development is hard. It takes discipline to make it all work, certainly more discipline than piling a bunch of programmers into the same cubicle farm.
I believe remote development represents the future of work.
The shared source code was the mutual glue that stuck us together, motivated us, and kept us moving forward.
key_insight: Remote development succeeds not through tools or process alone, but by assembling proven programmers who genuinely love code and giving them autonomy with clear leadership vision.
